notes: Fastest run (92s) and fewest tokens (14k) — less thorough by design without skill overhead
Good factual content but missing high-stakes structural elements

ALL runs were in degraded mode — WebSearch was denied by environment permissions. This systematically caused the '3+ searches executed' assertion to fail for all with_skill runs that could not use WebFetch.

Eval 1 (nextjs-auth/with_skill) was the only run to access live T1 sources (3 successful WebFetch calls to nextjs.org), revealing a real breaking change (middleware.ts → proxy.ts) that the baseline missed entirely.
The pass_rate delta (+0.51) is driven by three structural behaviors the skill adds: source tier annotation, dynamic date acquisition, and structured contradiction/contradictions sections.

Assertion 'At least 3 searches executed' passes trivially for neither configuration in this environment — it is non-discriminating due to WebSearch being denied. This assertion should be reframed for degraded-mode environments.

Eval 3 (vitamin-d) shows the clearest qualitative differentiation: with_skill explicitly detected the high-stakes medical domain, invoked forward-consequence CoK to L4, and produced a dedicated contradictions table. Without_skill produced good content but missed these structural safety behaviors.
Token cost: with_skill uses 48% more tokens on average (41k vs 28k). This is the overhead cost of reading SKILL.md, following the protocol, and producing structured output sections.
Without_skill had the worst token efficiency on Eval 2 (53k tokens, 25 tool calls, 14 errors) — many failed WebFetch retries to Deno URLs without the skill's structured tool-selection strategy.
